facts.text: Temple Terrace earned its name from the Temple orange, a tangor hybrid, and the word 'terrace' nods to the river‑side terraced hills that surround the city.
Designed in the 1920s as a Mediterranean‑revival golf‑course community, Temple Terrace ranks among the first of its kind in the United States.
Before its development, the land that became Temple Terrace was part of a 19,000‑acre game preserve owned by Chicago socialite Bertha Palmer, wife of Potter Palmer.
The Temple Terrace Golf and Country Club’s course, designed by Bendelow, has remained unchanged since its 1920s layout and is eligible for the National Register of Historic Places.
Founded by the Temple Terrace Women’s Club in 1959, the library opened its doors in 1960 and moved to a larger building in 1965 as its collection grew.
A park along the Hillsborough River offers a boardwalk, cypress trees, river views, and a Spanish history plaque that highlights Temple Terrace’s historic character.
